ollama_format.description: Structured smoke output schema for one text response.
ollama_format.title: StructuredSmokeText

structured_output_schema.description: Structured smoke output schema for one text response.
structured_output_schema.title: StructuredSmokeText

parsed_response.response_text: The number of households engaging in social learning remains constant at zero for the initial 14 timesteps, indicating no adoption of social learning strategies during the early phase of the simulation. At timestep 14, the count begins to rise gradually, reaching 21 by timestep 17 before plateauing temporarily at intermediate values. A significant upward trend emerges around timestep 23, where the count surges to 54, peaking at timestep 26 with 51 households actively engaging in social learning. From timestep 27 onward, the metric stabilizes at consistently high levels between 18 and 20 households, suggesting an equilibrium where social learning adoption balances across households. This pattern reflects adaptive behavioral responses to climatic variability (global-rain? enabled, mean rainfall 200, standard deviation 100), as agents likely adopt social learning to mitigate uncertainty in pasture management under unpredictable rainfall. The absence of strategy switching (SL2-strategy-switch? disabled) implies that the increase in social learning adoption stems from knowledge-radius-driven interactions (set to 1), rather than agents altering their behavioral types. The stabilization phase indicates a system where social learning becomes entrenched, potentially improving collective resource management resilience, though the data do not quantify how this literal impacts longer-term outcomes like pasture biomass or livestock survival due to the reported furceto. The findings align with the model’s focus on how learning mechanisms interact with homogeneous behavioral types under environmental stochasticity.

From timestep 14 onward, the count of households doing social learning increases steadily. For example, at timestep 14, it goes from 14 to 17, then 15 to 21, and so on. The numbers keep going up until they plateau around 17 or 18. Wait, no, looking at the last few lines, the numbers keep rising to 42, 50, etc. Wait, maybe I misread. Let me check again.

The user provided a data table with timesteps from 0 to 29 (since there are 30 columns after the first one). Wait, the first column is the timestep, starting at 0, up to 29.
